perturbation.original_text: Volunteer acknowledges that a violation of the terms of this Agreement may cause damage and harm to CCPDT and that any such damage or harm will be difficult if not impossible to calculate in monetary terms and will be irreparable to CCPDT. Volunteer agrees that, upon notice from CCPDT declaring a breach of this Agreement, Volunteer shall immediately cease all further activities which are, or are claimed by CCPDT to be, a breach of this Agreement. The Certification Council for Professional Dog Trainers may also avail itself of any other remedies available by law.
perturbation.changed_text: Volunteer acknowledges that a violation of the terms of this Agreement may cause damage and harm to CCPDT and that any such damage or harm will be difficult if not impossible to calculate in monetary terms and will be irreparable to CCPDT. Volunteer agrees that, upon notice from CCPDT declaring a breach of this Agreement, Volunteer shall immediately cease all further activities which are, or are claimed by CCPDT to be, a breach of this Agreement. The Certification Council for Professional Dog Trainers may only seek arbitration for damages, waiving all rights to legal remedies.
perturbation.explanation: Restricting remedies to only arbitration is an issue because it removes options the CCPDT has under the law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Federal Arbitration Act, 9 U.S.C. § 2, validates the enforceability of arbitration agreements, but does not mandate it as the *sole* remedy, so specifying that the only remedy is arbitration in this contract may contradict the FAA, which allows parties access to federal courts.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate U.S. Code Notes prev | next A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising out of such a contract, transaction, or refusal, shall be valid, irrevocable, and enforceable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract or as otherwise provided in chapter 4. (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 670 ; Pub. L. 117–90, § 2(b)(1)(A) , Mar. 3, 2022 , 136 Stat. 27 .) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, § 2, 43 Stat. 883 . Editorial Notes Amendments 2022— Pub. L. 117–90 inserted “or as otherwise provided in chapter 4” before period at end. Statutory Notes and Related Subsidiaries Effective Date of 2022 Amendment Amendment by Pub. L. 117–90 applicable with respect to any dispute or claim that arises or accrues on or after Mar. 3, 2022 , see section 3 of Pub. L. 117–90 , set out as an Effective Date note under section 401 of this title . U.S. Code Toolbox Law about... perturbation.justification: The modified text limits CCPDT to only seeking arbitration for damages, explicitly waiving all rights to other legal remedies available by law. This directly contradicts the principle that agreements to arbitrate are valid and enforceable but do not necessarily preclude access to other legal avenues. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing 9 U.S. Code § 2, states that agreements to arbitrate are "valid, irrevocable, and enforceable." It does not state or imply that they are the exclusive remedy, thus implying the existence of others. By explicitly waiving all other rights, the modified text undermines the availability of remedies beyond arbitration, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Volunteers shall act fairly and be impartial and unbiased in their service to the Certification Council for Professional Dog Trainers and to the population of Certificants and candidates.
perturbation.changed_text: Volunteers shall act in the best interests of the Certification Council for Professional Dog Trainers, even if it conflicts with fairness and impartiality to the population of Certificants and candidates.
perturbation.explanation: Encouraging bias goes against federal regulations about fair practices.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964
perturbation.law_explanation: While Title VII primarily addresses employment discrimination, the requirement for volunteers to act in the best interests of the CCPDT, even if it means being unfair or biased, could potentially lead to discriminatory practices in certification processes, indirectly conflicting with the spirit and intent of fair practices as promoted by Title VII (42 U.S.C. § 2000e-2). This is because volunteers who are involved in certification decisions might favor certain candidates or groups over others, thus influencing the certification process based on factors not related to merit or qualifications.

L. 88-352), as amended. Title VII is codified at 42 U.S.C. 2000e and in subsequent sections. Title VII amendments include those introduced by the Civil Rights Act of 1991 (CRA) and the Lilly Ledbetter Fair Pay Act of 2009 . Editor’s notes appear in italics and include cross-references to the codified version of Title VII. Title VII prohibits employment discrimination based on race, color, religion, sex and national origin. An Act To enforce the constitutional right to vote, to confer jurisdiction upon the district courts of the United States to provide injunctive relief against discrimination in public accommodations, to authorize the attorney General to institute suits to protect constitutional rights in public facilities and public education, to extend the Commission on Civil Rights, to prevent discrimination in federally assisted programs, to establish a Commission on Equal Employment Opportunity, and for other purposes. Be it enacted by the Senate and House of Representatives of the United States of America in Congress assembled, That this Act may be cited as the "Civil Rights Act of 1964". * * * DEFINITIONS SEC. 2000e. [Section 701] For the purposes of this subchapter- (a) The term "person" includes one or more individuals, governments, governmental agencies, political subdivisions, labor unions, partnerships, associations, corporations, legal representatives, mutual companies, joint-­stock companies, trusts, unincorporated organizations, trustees, trustees in cases under Title 11 [originally, bankruptcy ], or receivers. (b) The term "employer" means a person engaged in an industry affecting commerce who has fifteen or more employees for each working day in each of twenty or more calendar weeks in the current or preceding calendar year, and any agent of such a person, but such term does not include (1) the United States, a corporation wholly owned by the Government of the United States, an Indian tribe, or any department or agency of the District of Columbia subject by statute to procedures of the competitive s

perturbation.justification: The modified text explicitly prioritizes the interests of the Certification Council for Professional Dog Trainers (CCPDT) over fairness and impartiality to certificants and candidates. This directly contradicts the spirit and intent of fair practices, potentially leading to discriminatory certification processes. While the scraped snippet 1 focuses on employment discrimination under Title VII, the law explanation extends to the broader principle of fair practices, which the change undermines. The original text mandated fairness and impartiality; the change allows for bias. The scraped snippet 2 results in a 'Page Not Found' error and cannot be used to enhance the decision.

perturbation.original_text: Volunteer agrees to maintain the confidentiality of all Confidential Information and not misuse, misappropriate, or disclose in writing, orally or by electronic means, any Confidential Information, directly or indirectly, to any other person or use them in any way, either during the term of this Agreement or at any other time thereafter, except as is required in the course of service to the CCPDT.
perturbation.changed_text: Volunteer agrees to maintain the confidentiality of all Confidential Information and not misuse, misappropriate, or disclose in writing, orally or by electronic means, any Confidential Information, directly or indirectly, to any other person or use them in any way, either during the term of this Agreement or at any other time thereafter, except as is required in the course of service to the CCPDT, or if compelled by valid subpoena.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'or if compelled by valid subpoena' goes against compliance laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA)
perturbation.law_explanation: While seemingly compliant, broadly allowing disclosure under 'valid subpoena' without specifying adherence to HIPAA regulations (45 CFR § 164.512(e)) regarding protected health information can create a conflict. If the agreement's Confidential Information includes any health information, compliance with HIPAA is mandatory, which necessitates specific conditions and safeguards before disclosure, even under subpoena. The altered text's generality could lead to improper disclosures, especially if volunteers are not trained in HIPAA compliance.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a potential contradiction by broadly allowing disclosure under 'valid subpoena' without specifying adherence to HIPAA regulations, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references 45 CFR 164.512, which pertains to uses and disclosures not requiring authorization, suggesting that specific rules govern disclosures even under legal compulsion. The addition of 'or if compelled by valid subpoena' to the original confidentiality clause, without further qualification regarding HIPAA compliance, weakens the original obligation to maintain confidentiality. This general allowance could lead to improper disclosures of protected health information if the agreement involves such information and volunteers are not properly trained in HIPAA compliance, creating a conflict with HIPAA's stringent requirements for disclosure.
